case_name: Sh. YASHPAL CHAUDHRANI & ORS. vs STATE (GOVT. OF NCT DELHI) & ANR.
court: High Court of Delhi at New Delhi
facts: Five petitions under Section 482 Cr.P.C. sought quashing of criminal proceedings. Four petitions related to connected FIRs from 2003 (Special Cell) alleging large-scale credit-card cloning and fraud where HDFC Bank was the complainant and a court-annexed mediation settlement of Rs.12,00,000/- was recorded on 03.07.2018. The fifth petition related to obscene calls and alleged IT Act offences (FIR No.454/2013) where a settlement dated 19.11.2018 for Rs.2,00,000/- was recorded. The High Court reviewed extensive delay in prosecution, the mediation process, and precedents on quashing non-compoundable or serious offences.
arguments_claimant: The petitioners contended that the disputes had been amicably settled through court-referred mediation at the Delhi High Court Mediation & Conciliation Centre and therefore the criminal proceedings should be quashed under the inherent powers of the High Court (Section 482 Cr.P.C.). They relied on the fact of prolonged pendency (about 15 years) and the complainant bank's executed settlement (including delivery of instruments totalling around Rs.11.77 lakhs and cash) as justifications for ending the prosecutions.
arguments_respondent: The State strongly objected, relying on Supreme Court authorities (for example Parbatbhai Aahir) and arguing that the offences involved deep-rooted criminal conspiracy and economic frauds with serious public impact, not merely private disputes. The State and intervenors urged that the nature and gravity of the offences, antecedents of accused and public interest militate against quashing; the bank later sought to revoke its earlier 'no-objection' for quashing.
ratio_decidendi: "There is no bar to the disputant parties to be referred by the court to mediation, even in cases involving such non-compoundable offences the action in which context can be lawfully terminated by approaching the High Court under Section 482 Cr. PC, provided the parties are willing and there exists an element of settlement." The Court held, however, that "the court while considering reference of the parties to a criminal case to mediation must before even ascertaining as to whether elements of settlement exist first examine, by preliminary scrutiny, the permissibility in law for the criminal action to be brought to an end". Importantly the Court reiterated that the High Court "must steer clear of intervention in 'heinous' or 'serious' offences, including those involving 'mental depravity', as indeed 'economic offences' affecting 'the financial and economic well being of the State'... such offences are 'not private in nature' but have 'a serious impact upon society'". Applying these principles, the Court found the credit-card frauds and the obscene-calls/IT offences to be unacceptable for quashing on settlement given their gravity, public impact, and the accuseds' conduct.
final_order: All five petitions are dismissed. The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate was directed to take the four credit-card fraud matters on day-to-day basis, ensure formal charges are framed, enforce strict attendance of accused and counsel, the Sessions Judge to monitor progress and the matters to be disposed preferably within six months; the judgment is to be circulated to criminal courts and mediation centres.
summary: This is a Delhi High Court decision (Apr 22, 2019) on five petitions seeking quashing of criminal prosecutions after court-referred mediations produced monetary settlements (HDFC: Rs.12 lakh; obscene-calls matter: Rs.2 lakh). The central legal issue was whether mediation settlements can justify quashing under Section 482 Cr.P.C. for serious or economically significant offences. The Court held that while mediation referrals are not per se impermissible, courts and mediators must preliminarily scrutinize permissibility and must not permit quashing for 'heinous' or serious economic crimes; the petitions were dismissed and active directions were given to expedite trial proceedings.
embedding_summary: Sh. Yashpal Chaudhrani & Ors. v. State (Delhi HC, 2019, AIRONLINE 2019 DEL 585; CRL.M.C. 5765/2018 & connected matters) concerns five S.482 CrPC petitions challenging prosecution: four connected 2003 Special Cell FIRs for large-scale credit-card cloning and fraud (complainant HDFC Bank) with a mediation settlement of Rs.12,00,000/- dated 03.07.2018, and one 2013 Amar Colony FIR under IT Act (66A/67/67A) with settlement of Rs.2,00,000/-. Key statutes considered: Section 482 Cr.P.C., Section 320 Cr.P.C., Section 89 CPC, Section 138 NI Act, Sections 66A/67 IT Act and multiple IPC provisions. Core legal issues: validity of court-referred mediation in criminal/ non-compoundable cases, scope of quashing under Section 482, public interest in serious/economic offences, and obligations on courts/mediators to vet settlements. The Court applied Supreme Court precedents (Parbatbhai Aahir, Gian Singh, Narinder Singh, Afcons, et al.), held settlements unacceptable on these facts, dismissed petitions, and directed day-to-day trial progression and institutional safeguards for mediation centres.
legal_principles: High Court's inherent jurisdiction under Section 482 Cr.P.C. may quash proceedings to 'secure the ends of justice' or 'prevent abuse of the process of any court' but must be exercised sparingly.
Court must 'steer clear of intervention in "heinous" or "serious" offences' and those 'not private in nature' including economic/financial frauds that have 'a serious impact upon society'.
Criminal cases may be referred to mediation, but the referring court must undertake preliminary scrutiny of permissibility before making a reference.

Mediators and mediation centres must vet criminal-case settlements to ensure they relate to offences which are compoundable or quashable under Section 482 and must not broker incompatible compromises.

Cases of predominantly civil/commercial character (e.g., certain financial disputes or cheque bounce cases) may be quashed where conviction is remote and continuation would cause oppression/prejudice.

The High Court may examine evidence to see if serious offence labels are used merely 'for the sake of it', but such scrutiny is generally inappropriate until sufficient investigation/charge-sheeting has been done.
sections_cited: Section 482. Saving of inherent powers of High Court (text reproduced in judgment)
Section 89 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908

Four petitions related to connected FIRs from 2003 (Special Cell) alleging large-scale credit-card cloning and fraud where HDFC Bank was the complainant and a court-annexed mediation settlement of Rs.12,00,000/- was recorded on 03.07.2018. The fifth petition related to obscene calls and alleged IT Act offences (FIR No.454/2013) where a settlement dated 19.11.2018 for Rs.2,00,000/- was recorded. The High Court reviewed extensive delay in prosecution, the mediation process, and precedents on quashing non-compoundable or serious offences. The petitioners contended that the disputes had been amicably settled through court-referred mediation at the Delhi High Court Mediation & Conciliation Centre and therefore the criminal proceedings should be quashed under the inherent powers of the High Court (Section 482 Cr.P.C.). They relied on the fact of prolonged pendency (about 15 years) and the complainant bank's executed settlement (including delivery of instruments totalling around Rs.11.77 lakhs and cash) as justifications for ending the prosecutions. The State strongly objected, relying on Supreme Court authorities (for example Parbatbhai Aahir) and arguing that the offences involved deep-rooted criminal conspiracy and economic frauds with serious public impact, not merely private disputes. The State and intervenors urged that the nature and gravity of the offences, antecedents of accused and public interest militate against quashing; the bank later sought to revoke its earlier 'no-objection' for quashing. "There is no bar to the disputant parties to be referred by the court to mediation, even in cases involving such non-compoundable offences the action in which context can be lawfully terminated by approaching the High Court under Section 482 Cr. PC, provided the parties are willing and there exists an element of settlement." The Court held, however, that "the court while considering reference of the parties to a criminal case to mediation must before even ascertaining as to whether elements of settlement exist first examine, by preliminary scrutiny, the permissibility in law for the criminal action to be brought to an end". Importantly the Court reiterated that the High Court "must steer clear of intervention in 'heinous' or 'serious' offences, including those involving 'mental depravity', as indeed 'economic offences' affecting 'the financial and economic well being of the State'... such offences are 'not private in nature' but have 'a serious impact upon society'". Applying these principles, the Court found the credit-card frauds and the obscene-calls/IT offences to be unacceptable for quashing on settlement given their gravity, public impact, and the accuseds' conduct. All five petitions are dismissed. The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate was directed to take the four credit-card fraud matters on day-to-day basis, ensure formal charges are framed, enforce strict attendance of accused and counsel, the Sessions Judge to monitor progress and the matters to be disposed preferably within six months; the judgment is to be circulated to criminal courts and mediation centres. The central legal issue was whether mediation settlements can justify quashing under Section 482 Cr.P.C. for serious or economically significant offences. The Court held that while mediation referrals are not per se impermissible, courts and mediators must preliminarily scrutinize permissibility and must not permit quashing for 'heinous' or serious economic crimes; the petitions were dismissed and active directions were given to expedite trial proceedings. Sh. Yashpal Chaudhrani & Ors. v. State (Delhi HC, 2019, AIRONLINE 2019 DEL 585; CRL.M.C. 5765/2018 & connected matters) concerns five S.482 CrPC petitions challenging prosecution: four connected 2003 Special Cell FIRs for large-scale credit-card cloning and fraud (complainant HDFC Bank) with a mediation settlement of Rs.12,00,000/- dated 03.07.2018, and one 2013 Amar Colony FIR under IT Act (66A/67/67A) with settlement of Rs.2,00,000/-. Key statutes considered: Section 482 Cr.P.C., Section 320 Cr.P.C., Section 89 CPC, Section 138 NI Act, Sections 66A/67 IT Act and multiple IPC provisions. Core legal issues: validity of court-referred mediation in criminal/ non-compoundable cases, scope of quashing under Section 482, public interest in serious/economic offences, and obligations on courts/mediators to vet settlements. The Court applied Supreme Court precedents (Parbatbhai Aahir, Gian Singh, Narinder Singh, Afcons, et al.), held settlements unacceptable on these facts, dismissed petitions, and directed day-to-day trial progression and institutional safeguards for mediation centres. High Court's inherent jurisdiction under Section 482 Cr.P.C. may quash proceedings to 'secure the ends of justice' or 'prevent abuse of the process of any court' but must be exercised sparingly. Court must 'steer clear of intervention in "heinous" or "serious" offences' and those 'not private in nature' including economic/financial frauds that have 'a serious impact upon society'. Criminal cases may be referred to mediation, but the referring court must undertake preliminary scrutiny of permissibility before making a reference. Mediators and mediation centres must vet criminal-case settlements to ensure they relate to offences which are compoundable or quashable under Section 482 and must not broker incompatible compromises. Cases of predominantly civil/commercial character (e.g., certain financial disputes or cheque bounce cases) may be quashed where conviction is remote and continuation would cause oppression/prejudice. The High Court may examine evidence to see if serious offence labels are used merely 'for the sake of it', but such scrutiny is generally inappropriate until sufficient investigation/charge-sheeting has been done.
